angle.headline: Why Mexico's Growth Stagnation Is an Operating Model Problem, Not a Policy Problem
angle.thesis: Mexico has tried every policy fix—privatization, nationalization, trade agreements, PhD-led governments—yet GDP growth remains stuck at 2% because the country suffers from the same dysfunction as mid-market owners: high complexity without the operating architecture to convert activity into productive output.
angle.targetPersona: US-based owners expanding into Mexico or LATAM operators watching nearshoring promises fail to materialize in their own revenue growth despite record-breaking macro indicators
angle.relevanceToAudience: The corridor operator sees the contradiction everywhere: Mexico hits records in FDI, remittances, and tax collection, yet their own business can't hire talent, can't scale operations, and can't convert proximity into margin. The macro story and the operating reality have diverged—exactly like their own company before they fixed the operating model.
talkingPoints: Mexico proves all the macro inputs—FDI records, nearshoring momentum, USMCA advantages—mean nothing without the operating architecture to convert them into productive output

The 'stagnation trap' diagnosis applies equally to countries and companies: 55% informality, weak institutions, and segmented labor markets are the national version of 'we can't function without the owner in every decision'

The framework is Operating Model OS: when you build complexity (industrial capacity, trade infrastructure, multi-market footprint) before you build the machine that runs it, growth stalls regardless of capital or opportunity

The action is the same at every scale—audit where you're adding inputs (investment, headcount, tools) without first building the repeatable processes, role clarity, and accountability systems that convert inputs into leverage

enContent.subject: Your succession plan is hiring for a role that doesn't exist
enContent.preheader: Succession is not a people problem. It is an operating model problem.
subjectOptions: The operating model survives the person
Your succession plan is hiring for a role that doesn't exist
Meta cut 8,000. What your operating model owes now.

sections.heading: The Signal
sections.body:
*This week: the operating model is what keeps running when the person, the headcount, or the assumption disappears.*

- **Strategy:** Mexican imports of Chinese footwear fell 62% and textiles 26% in the first two months of 2026, after tariffs up to 35% and IMMEX restrictions took effect. Imports of footwear parts rose 104%.
  **Strategy is the discipline of naming which sourcing assumptions were contracts and which were conveniences.**
  [Read ->](https://expansion.mx/economia/2026/04/23/menos-china-en-mexico-aranceles-hunden-importaciones-calzado)
- **Operating Models:** Emilio Azcárraga Jean cut his Televisa stake from 46.7% to 23.5% and ceded voting control. Bernardo Gómez and Alfonso de Angoitia now run the company.
  **An operating model is what keeps running after the founder stops running it.**
  [Read ->](https://expansion.mx/empresas/2026/04/23/emilio-azcarraga-jean-dejo-televisa-cedio-control-quien-dirige-actualmente)
- **Technology:** 51% of companies surveyed by LangChain now have AI agents in production, according to the State of AI Agents report.
  **Technology amplifies what is written down and erases what is not.**
  [Read ->](https://expansion.mx/opinion/2026/04/23/de-los-chatbots-a-superagentes-cuando-la-ia-dejo-de-hablar-y-se-puso-a-trabajar)
- **Human Capital:** Meta will lay off 8,000 people, 10% of its global workforce, on May 20 and cancel 6,000 planned hires. The cuts are framed as efficiency to offset AI investment.
  **Human capital planning is the decision about which work survives the next round of efficiency.**
  [Read ->](https://elpais.com/economia/2026-04-23/meta-despedira-a-cerca-de-8000-personas-el-10-de-su-plantilla-global-por-el-impacto-de-sus-inversiones-en-ia.html)

sections.heading: The Apertura
sections.body:
===OPTION_A:observation===
A client called me Tuesday. His VP of operations had given notice that morning. Fifteen years in the role. By the end of the day he had sent me a list of twenty-three things that would need to be figured out in the next forty-five days. Vendor relationships, client escalation rules, the logic behind his inventory thresholds. None of it written down. He was not asking me to help him find a replacement. He had already called a recruiter. He was asking me something harder: what is the actual job we are hiring for, when nobody here has ever seen it written down?
===OPTION_B:provocation===
Succession is not a people problem. Most of the owners I work with think it is. They read the articles, they build the bench, they run the development plans. Then the person leaves and nothing works, and they conclude they picked the wrong replacement. They did not pick the wrong replacement. They picked a person to fill a role that was never a role. It was a person-shaped hole that one individual had been patching for years with their own judgment. The next person was set up to fail before they signed the offer letter.
===OPTION_C:pattern===
Three conversations this month. Different industries. Same detail at minute twelve. 'We just lost someone critical and we are realizing how much they were holding together.' One was a head of finance. One was a plant manager. One was an executive assistant who had been there eighteen years. In all three cases the owner described the role the same way. 'Only she knew how to do that.' That sentence is the diagnosis. It tells you the job was never documented. It tells you the business was running on a person, not a system. It tells you what comes next.

sections.heading: The Insight
sections.body:
You think succession is a people problem. Who replaces your head of ops. Who takes over if your sales director leaves. Who could step in if you took ninety days off. You treat it as recruiting. Find the right person, put them in the seat, and the function continues.

Most advisors will agree with this framing. They will help you build bench strength, run development plans, design leadership tracks. None of it holds when the person actually leaves. The replacement walks in and nothing works the way it worked two weeks ago. You conclude you picked the wrong person. You did not pick the wrong person. You picked a person to fill a role that was never a role.

What you are actually losing when someone leaves is not their skills. It is the undocumented decisions they made each week. The standards they applied in their head. The relationships they maintained without a system. The routines that existed because they existed. The next hire inherits a ghost, not a function.

**The person-proof test**

The Operating Model OS has a simple diagnostic for this, what I call the person-proof test. For any critical role, ask one question: if this person left Monday with no notice, what specifically would stop working by Friday? The list is your operating model debt. It is the work that never got converted from person to system.

The debt lives in three layers. First, the recurring decisions. What does this role decide each week, on what inputs, by what rule? Second, the standards. What does 'good' look like in this function, and who gets looped in when something exceeds normal? Third, the handoffs. Who feeds this role and who receives from it?

An operating model that passes the person-proof test does not need heroes. That is the point. Not because people stop mattering. They matter more, because you finally have a structure they can contribute to instead of one they have to carry. When people leave, the machine keeps running. When you hire, the new person steps into a defined role instead of a person-shaped hole.

**This week**

Pick one critical function in your business. Not the one you are most worried about. The one you rely on most without thinking about it. Write down the three recurring decisions it makes. Not what you want it to do. What it actually does on a typical Tuesday. If you cannot write that down in one sitting, you have just diagnosed your first operating model gap. That gap is not a crisis. It is the starting point.

sections.heading: The Field Report
sections.body:
Meta will lay off 8,000 people on May 20. That is 10% of its global workforce. The company is also cancelling 6,000 planned hires. According to [El País reporting on the internal note](https://elpais.com/economia/2026-04-23/meta-despedira-a-cerca-de-8000-personas-el-10-de-su-plantilla-global-por-el-impacto-de-sus-inversiones-en-ia.html), the cuts are framed as an efficiency effort to offset the company's AI investments.

The headline reads as a tech story. It is not. It is an operating model story. Meta is betting that its AI investments will absorb work that humans were doing. That bet only works if the work being absorbed was already documented. You cannot hand a function to a model if the function lived in the individual doing it.

For US-based owners watching this: the question is not whether you will face the same pressure. You will. The question is whether your operating model is written down clearly enough for AI to take parts of it, or for a smaller team to run it. Meta's layoffs are not the warning. Meta's bet is. They are betting their documentation is good enough. Are you betting yours is?

sections.heading: The Tool
sections.body: **Granola.** An AI notepad that captures meeting decisions and action items automatically in the background. Why it matters to a $5M to $100M operator: the first obstacle to documenting your operating model is finding the decisions in the first place. Granola turns every recurring meeting into a searchable record of what was decided and by whom. [granola.ai](https://granola.ai)

sections.heading: The Compass
sections.body: **Watch for this week:** which senior people on your team have cancelled, moved, or quietly shortened their upcoming time off. The ones who cannot take a clean week away are the ones whose work has not been converted from person to system. Their calendar is your operating model diagnostic. If three people on your senior team cannot take a week off in the next quarter, that is the list of roles that would break if they left.

sections.heading: The Door
sections.body:
If something in this issue landed, reply - I read every response.

If this is useful to someone in your network, forward it - it takes ten seconds and it's the highest compliment.

When you're ready to work together directly, here is how we start: [link]

esContent.subject: Su plan de sucesión está contratando para un puesto que no existe
esContent.preheader: La sucesión no es un problema de personas. Es un problema de modelo operativo.
esContent.thesis: México ha intentado cada receta de política pública: privatización, nacionalización, T-MEC, gobiernos de doctores. Y el PIB sigue atorado en 2%. El país carga la misma disfunción que el empresario mediano: mucha complejidad y muy poca arquitectura operativa para convertir la actividad en resultado.

sections.heading: LA SEÑAL
sections.body:
*Esta semana: el modelo operativo es lo que sigue funcionando cuando la persona, la plaza o el supuesto desaparecen.*

- **Estrategia:** Las importaciones mexicanas de calzado chino cayeron 62% y las de textiles 26% en los primeros dos meses de 2026, tras aranceles de hasta 35% y restricciones al IMMEX. Las importaciones de partes de calzado subieron 104%.
  **La estrategia es la disciplina de distinguir cuáles supuestos de abastecimiento eran contratos y cuáles eran solo conveniencias.**
  [Leer ->](https://expansion.mx/economia/2026/04/23/menos-china-en-mexico-aranceles-hunden-importaciones-calzado)
- **Modelos Operativos:** Emilio Azcárraga Jean redujo su participación en Televisa de 46.7% a 23.5% y cedió el control del voto. Bernardo Gómez y Alfonso de Angoitia ahora dirigen la empresa.
  **Un modelo operativo es lo que sigue funcionando después de que el fundador deja de operarlo.**
  [Leer ->](https://expansion.mx/empresas/2026/04/23/emilio-azcarraga-jean-dejo-televisa-cedio-control-quien-dirige-actualmente)
- **Tecnología:** El 51% de las empresas encuestadas por LangChain ya tiene agentes de IA en producción, según el reporte State of AI Agents.
  **La tecnología amplifica lo que está escrito y borra lo que no.**
  [Leer ->](https://expansion.mx/opinion/2026/04/23/de-los-chatbots-a-superagentes-cuando-la-ia-dejo-de-hablar-y-se-puso-a-trabajar)
- **Capital Humano:** Meta despedirá a 8,000 personas el 20 de mayo, el 10% de su plantilla global, y cancelará 6,000 contrataciones planeadas. Los recortes se presentan como eficiencia para compensar la inversión en IA.
  **La planeación de capital humano es la decisión sobre qué trabajo sobrevive a la próxima ronda de eficiencia.**
  [Leer ->](https://elpais.com/economia/2026-04-23/meta-despedira-a-cerca-de-8000-personas-el-10-de-su-plantilla-global-por-el-impacto-de-sus-inversiones-en-ia.html)

sections.heading: LA APERTURA
sections.body: Un cliente de años me llamó el martes, con esa voz que uno reconoce cuando algo se rompió temprano. Su director de operaciones había renunciado esa mañana. Quince años en el puesto. Para la tarde me había mandado una lista de veintitrés asuntos que habría que resolver en los próximos cuarenta y cinco días. Relaciones con proveedores. Reglas para escalar quejas de clientes. La lógica detrás de sus niveles de inventario. Nada de eso estaba por escrito. No me pedía ayuda para encontrar un reemplazo. Ya había llamado a una cazatalentos. Me estaba preguntando algo más difícil: ¿cuál es el puesto que en realidad estamos contratando, si nadie aquí lo ha visto nunca documentado?

sections.heading: EL INSIGHT
sections.body:
Usted cree que la sucesión es un problema de personas. Quién reemplaza a su director de operaciones. Quién toma el lugar del director comercial si se va. Quién podría entrar si usted se tomara noventa días. Lo trata como reclutamiento. Encontrar al indicado, sentarlo en la silla y la función continúa.

La mayoría de los asesores estará de acuerdo con ese marco. Le ayudarán a construir banca de talento, correr planes de desarrollo, diseñar rutas de liderazgo. Nada de eso aguanta cuando la persona de verdad se va. El reemplazo entra y nada funciona como funcionaba dos semanas antes. Usted concluye que escogió mal. No escogió mal. Escogió a alguien para ocupar un puesto que nunca fue un puesto.

Lo que de verdad pierde cuando alguien se va no son las habilidades. Son las decisiones no documentadas que esa persona tomaba cada semana. Los estándares que aplicaba en su cabeza. Las relaciones que mantenía sin un sistema. Las rutinas que existían porque ella existía. El siguiente contratado hereda un fantasma, no una función.

**La prueba a prueba de persona**

El Operating Model OS tiene un diagnóstico sencillo para esto, lo que llamo la prueba a prueba de persona. Para cualquier puesto crítico, haga una sola pregunta: si esta persona se fuera el lunes sin aviso, ¿qué dejaría de funcionar para el viernes? Esa lista es su deuda de modelo operativo. Es el trabajo que nunca se convirtió de persona a sistema.

La deuda vive en tres capas. Primero, las decisiones recurrentes. ¿Qué decide este puesto cada semana, con qué insumos, bajo qué regla? Segundo, los estándares. ¿Cómo se ve lo bueno en esta función y a quién se le avisa cuando algo sale de lo normal? Tercero, los relevos. ¿Quién alimenta a este puesto y quién recibe de él?

Un modelo operativo que pasa la prueba a prueba de persona no necesita héroes. Ese es el punto. No porque las personas dejen de importar. Importan más, porque por fin tienen una estructura a la que pueden contribuir, en vez de una que tienen que cargar. Cuando alguien se va, la máquina sigue funcionando. Cuando usted contrata, la persona nueva entra a un puesto definido, no a un hueco con forma de persona.

**Esta semana**

Escoja una función crítica de su negocio. No la que más le preocupa. La que más usa sin pensarlo. Escriba las tres decisiones recurrentes que toma. No lo que usted quiere que haga. Lo que de hecho hace un martes cualquiera. Si no puede escribirlo de una sentada, acaba de diagnosticar su primera brecha de modelo operativo. Esa brecha no es una crisis. Es el punto de partida.

sections.heading: EL REPORTE DE CAMPO
sections.body:
Meta despedirá a 8,000 personas el 20 de mayo. Es el 10% de su plantilla global. La empresa también cancelará 6,000 contrataciones planeadas. Según [el reporte de El País sobre la nota interna](https://elpais.com/economia/2026-04-23/meta-despedira-a-cerca-de-8000-personas-el-10-de-su-plantilla-global-por-el-impacto-de-sus-inversiones-en-ia.html), los recortes se presentan como una medida de eficiencia para compensar las inversiones en IA.

El titular se lee como noticia tecnológica. No lo es. Es una noticia de modelo operativo. Meta apuesta a que su inversión en IA absorberá trabajo que antes hacían humanos. Esa apuesta solo funciona si el trabajo que se absorbe ya estaba documentado. Usted no puede entregarle una función a un modelo si la función vivía en la cabeza de quien la hacía.

Para el dueño mexicano que ve esto desde el corredor, la pregunta no es si enfrentará la misma presión. La va a enfrentar. La pregunta es si su modelo operativo está escrito con la claridad suficiente para que la IA tome partes, o para que un equipo más chico lo opere. Los despidos de Meta no son la advertencia. La apuesta de Meta sí lo es. Están apostando a que su documentación alcanza. ¿Usted está apostando lo mismo?

sections.heading: LA HERRAMIENTA
sections.body: **Granola.** Una libreta con IA que captura decisiones y tareas de cada junta en segundo plano, sin que usted tenga que tomar notas. Por qué importa para un operador de entre $5M y $100M: el primer obstáculo para documentar su modelo operativo es encontrar las decisiones. Granola convierte cada junta recurrente en un registro buscable de qué se decidió y quién lo decidió. [granola.ai](https://granola.ai)

sections.heading: LA BRÚJULA
sections.body: **Observe esta semana:** cuáles de sus directivos han cancelado, movido o acortado en silencio sus próximas vacaciones. Los que no logran tomarse una semana limpia son aquellos cuyo trabajo no se ha convertido de persona a sistema. Su calendario es el diagnóstico de su modelo operativo. Si tres personas de su equipo directivo no pueden salir una semana en el próximo trimestre, esa es la lista de puestos que se romperían si se fueran.

sections.heading: LA PUERTA
sections.body:
Si algo de este número resonó contigo, respóndeme - leo cada mensaje.
Si le es útil a alguien en tu red, reenvíalo - es el mayor cumplido.
Cuando estés listo para trabajar juntos directamente, así es como empezamos: [link]

issues.message: Signal word count is 197 — target range is 95–185.

The term 'person-proof test' is presented as a recognized framework ('The Operating Model OS has a simple diagnostic for this, what I call the person-proof test') but is not flagged as new in reviewFlags. Should either be flagged as writer's framing or replaced with established term like 'single point of failure analysis' or 'knowledge transfer audit'.
issues.excerpt: The Operating Model OS has a simple diagnostic for this, what I call the person-proof test.

issues.message: The term 'operating model debt' is introduced without attribution and not flagged. This could be reframed using established terms like 'institutional knowledge gap' or 'undocumented processes'.
issues.excerpt: The list is your operating model debt. It is the work that never got converted from person to system.

You treat it as recruiting. Find the right person, put them in the seat, and the function continues. [...] You did not pick the wrong person. You picked a person to fill a role that was never a role."
